title: The Magicians of Caprona — Chapter 1 (Fragment 3)
sourceText:
“I don’t think the Casa Petrocchi can be anything like this inside.”

Before Tonino could agree, they were swooped on lovingly by Aunt Maria, who was fatter than Aunt Gina, but not as fat as Aunt Anna. “Where have you been, my loves? I’ve been ready for your lessons for half an hour or more!”

Everyone in the Casa Montana worked very hard. Paolo and Tonino were already being taught the first rules for making spells. When Aunt Maria was busy, they were taught by their father, Antonio. Antonio was the eldest son of Old Niccolo, and would be head of the Casa Montana when Old Niccolo died. Paolo thought this weighed on his father. Antonio was a thin, worried person who laughed less often than the other Montanas. He was different. One of the differences was that, instead of letting Old Niccolo carefully choose a wife for him from a spell-house in Italy, Antonio had gone on a visit to England and come back married to Elizabeth. Elizabeth taught the boys music.

“If I’d been teaching that Angelica Petrocchi,” she was fond of saying, “she’d never have turned anything green.”

Old Niccolo said Elizabeth was the best musician in Caprona. And that, Lucia told the boys, was why Antonio got away with marrying her. But Rosa told them to take no notice. Rosa was proud of being half English.

Paolo and Tonino were probably prouder to be Montanas. It was a grand thing to know you were born into a family that was known world-wide as the greatest spell-house in Europe—if you did not count the Petrocchis. There were times when Paolo could hardly wait to grow up and be like his cousin, dashing Rinaldo. Everything came easily to Rinaldo. Girls fell in love with him, spells dripped from his pen. He had composed seven new charms before he left school. And these days, as Old Niccolo said, making a new spell was not easy. There were so many already. Paolo admired Rinaldo desperately. He told Tonino that Rinaldo was a true Montana.

Tonino agreed, because he was more than a year younger than Paolo and valued Paolo’s opinions, but it always seemed to him that it was Paolo who was the true Montana. Paolo was as quick as Rinaldo. He could learn without trying spells which took Tonino days to acquire. Tonino was slow. He could only remember things if he went over them again and again. It seemed to him that Paolo had been born with an instinct for magic which he just did not have himself.

Tonino was sometimes quite depressed about his slowness. Nobody else minded in the least. All his sisters, even the studious Corinna, spent hours helping him. Elizabeth assured him he never sang out of tune. His father scolded him for working too hard, and Paolo assured him that he would be streets ahead of the other children when he went to school. Paolo had just started school. He was as quick at ordinary lessons as he was at spells.

But when Tonino started school, he was just as slow there as he was at home. School bewildered him. He did not understand what the teachers wanted him to do. By the first Saturday he was so miserable that he had to slip away from the Casa and wander around Caprona in tears. He was missing for hours.

words.definition: To move rapidly downward through the air.
words.example: They were swooped on lovingly by Aunt Maria.
words.exampleText: They were swooped on lovingly by Aunt Maria.

words.definition: Of greatest age; oldest.
words.example: Antonio was the eldest son of Old Niccolo.
words.exampleText: Antonio was the eldest son of Old Niccolo.

words.definition: A family or establishment specializing in magic or spells.
words.example: The greatest spell-house in Europe.
words.exampleText: The greatest spell-house in Europe.

words.definition: To create or write (especially music).
words.example: He had composed seven new charms before he left school.
words.exampleText: He had composed seven new charms before he left school.

words.definition: Objects or words believed to have magical powers.
words.example: Spells dripped from his pen; he created charms.
words.exampleText: Spells dripped from his pen. He had composed seven new charms.

words.definition: An innate, usually fixed pattern of behaviour.
words.example: Paolo had been born with an instinct for magic.
words.exampleText: Paolo had been born with an instinct for magic.

words.definition: In a state of unhappiness or despondency.
words.example: Tonino was sometimes quite depressed about his slowness.
words.exampleText: Tonino was sometimes quite depressed about his slowness.

words.definition: Spending a lot of time studying or reading.
words.example: Even the studious Corinna spent hours helping him.
words.exampleText: Even the studious Corinna spent hours helping him.

words.definition: To tell someone confidently to remove doubt.
words.example: Paolo assured him he would be ahead of other children.
words.exampleText: Paolo assured him he would be streets ahead of the other children.

words.definition: Confused or puzzled completely.
words.example: School bewildered him.
words.exampleText: School bewildered him.

words.definition: Feeling very unhappy or uncomfortable.
words.example: He was so miserable he wandered around in tears.
words.exampleText: By the first Saturday he was so miserable that he had to slip away.

words.definition: Feeling deep pleasure or satisfaction as a result of achievements.
words.example: Rosa was proud of being half English.
words.exampleText: Rosa was proud of being half English.

words.definition: Magnificent and imposing in appearance or style.
words.example: It was a grand thing to know you belonged to a famous family.
words.exampleText: It was a grand thing to know you were born into a famous family.

words.definition: A child of one's aunt or uncle.
words.example: Paolo could hardly wait to be like his cousin Rinaldo.
words.exampleText: Paolo could hardly wait to grow up and be like his cousin, Rinaldo.

words.definition: In a way showing great urgency or desire.
words.example: Paolo admired Rinaldo desperately.
words.exampleText: Paolo admired Rinaldo desperately.

words.definition: Considered important or beneficial; appreciated.
words.example: Tonino valued Paolo’s opinions.
words.exampleText: Tonino valued Paolo’s opinions.

words.definition: To gain knowledge or skill by learning or experience.
words.example: Spells which took Tonino days to acquire.
words.exampleText: He could learn spells which took Tonino days to acquire.

words.definition: The quality of being slow; delayed speed or progress.
words.example: Tonino was depressed about his slowness.
words.exampleText: Tonino was sometimes quite depressed about his slowness.

words.definition: To reprimand or express sharp disapproval.
words.example: His father scolded him for working too hard.
words.exampleText: His father scolded him for working too hard.

words.definition: To ignore; pay no attention to.
words.example: Rosa told them to take no notice.
words.exampleText: Rosa told them to take no notice.

words.definition: Extending or reaching throughout the world.
words.example: Known world-wide as the greatest spell-house.
words.exampleText: Known world-wide as the greatest spell-house.

words.definition: In a rich, elaborate, or luxurious manner.
words.example: They were swooped on lovingly.
words.exampleText: They were swooped on lovingly by Aunt Maria.

words.definition: Loaded with a heavy weight or responsibility.
words.example: Paolo thought this weighed on his father.
words.exampleText: Paolo thought this weighed on his father.

words.definition: The state or feeling of being calm and in control of oneself.
words.example: Despite the pressure, Antonio maintained composure.
words.exampleText: Antonio was a worried person who laughed less often.

words.definition: A deep, persistent sadness.
words.example: He sometimes felt a sense of melancholy at school.
words.exampleText: He was miserable and bewildered at school.
